one_line_takeaway: SafeNeuron hardens LLM/VLM alignment against neuron-pruning attacks by freezing identified safety neurons and DPO-retraining the rest to grow redundant, distributed safety pathways -- cutting post-pruning attack-success rate several-fold (e.g. 56 vs 255/313 on Qwen2.5-14B) with negligible capability loss.
key_finding: Freezing already-identified safety neurons and retraining the rest yields safety that survives neuron removal far better than standard RLHF/DPO. On Qwen2.5-14B the union-removal ASR FULL(SN-) drops to 56/313 for SafeNeuron vs 255/313 for RLHF-Safety and 270/313 for the base model; on LLaMA-3.2-8B FULL(SN-) is 54/313 vs 142/313 (RLHF) vs 221/313 (base). The iterative scheme on Qwen2.5-7B monotonically lowers ASR 279 -> 183 -> 169 -> 158 out of 313 across T0-T3 while the frozen safety-neuron count grows 3717 -> 4848 -> 4953 -> 5218 and ARC/GSM8K/TruthfulQA stay essentially flat. Robustness transfers to vision: on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, SafeNeuron keeps ORI ASR at 1/313 (VL-Question) and 6/313 (NSFW) and FULL(SN-) at 106/313 and 148/313 vs RLHF-Safety's 152/313 and 149/313 -- despite safety neurons being identified purely from text, indicating largely modality-agnostic safety representations. Cross-task overlap of safety neurons converges to a stable ~16.8% core as tasks grow from a few (~53.2%) to ten, and safety neurons concentrate in middle/late layers across model scales, supporting the 'shared safety subspace' claim.
core_contribution: SafeNeuron is a neuron-level safety-alignment framework for LLMs and VLMs with two novel components proposed by the authors themselves (distinct from the cited SN-Tune / NeuronTune / NeuRel / FGSN line of work). (1) A training-free safety-neuron identification method combining two statistics computed over up- and gate-projection activations of GLU-FFN blocks: Activation Effect Size (ES), a pooled-std standardized mean difference between activations on harmful (Dunsafe) vs benign (Dsafe) prompts capturing distributional separability, and Safety Activation Shift (SAS), a layer-wise z-normalized directional shift capturing selectively amplified activation under harmful inputs; the final safety-neuron set is their union SN = SN_ES U SN_SAS. (2) A 'safety path redundancy' training strategy that FREEZES the identified safety neurons (sets their gradient to zero) and runs DPO preference optimization only on the remaining parameters, forcing the model to re-establish safety behavior through NEW, redundant neurons rather than reusing the sparse fragile pathway. An iterative variant re-identifies and freezes newly formed safety neurons each round to progressively distribute safety across the network. The central thesis: robust alignment requires distributed/redundant safety representations, not amplification of a few safety-critical units.
threat_model: Open-source (white-box) aligned LLMs/VLMs are the asset. The adversary can perform neuron-level attacks -- pruning, deactivating, or editing the small subset of safety-critical neurons -- to strip alignment and repurpose the model as a red-team / harmful-content generator, because behavioral alignment (SFT/RLHF/DPO) concentrates safety in few units (the paper cites the finding that removing ~5% of neurons can destroy up to ~90% of safety capability). The paper also considers standard jailbreak prompts (StrongREJECT / DAN / AdvBench) and multimodal attacks: FigStep-style typographic jailbreaks that render malicious text onto images (VL-Question) and directly-fed NSFW images with a neutral caption prompt. SafeNeuron is a DEFENSE aiming to keep ASR low even after an attacker removes the model's own safety neurons.
limitations: The pruning-robustness 'attack' removes exactly the neurons the method itself identifies (ES/SAS/union) rather than a third-party adaptive attacker, so FULL(SN-) is a self-consistent stress test, not an independent adversary; an attacker using a different identification method is untested. Even the best defense leaves large residual ASR under removal on several models (e.g. 137-183/313), so alignment is hardened, not guaranteed. The method essentially fails on the reasoning model DeepSeek-R1-1.5B, which stays highly unsafe (ORI ASR ~257/313 even for SafeNeuron, barely different from base 278/313). Identification needs white-box activations and curated Dsafe/Dunsafe sets plus two hand-set thresholds (tau_ES, tau_SAS). Theorem 3.1 is only an asymptotic (n->inf) bound under a null of equal means and does not bound false-negatives or finite-sample behavior. The paper has no dedicated limitations section and a generic boilerplate Impact Statement; multimodal evaluation is a single VLM backbone, and the LLaMA-3.2-8B label is anomalous (LLaMA-3.2 has no 8B variant, likely a mislabeling of LLaMA-3.1-8B).

datasets_used: StrongREJECT (313 malicious prompts incl. DAN, AdvBench; safety/ASR eval)
HarmfulQA (ten harmful-task categories; cross-task safety-neuron overlap analysis)
deepghs/nsfw_detect (NSFW images for VLM safety eval)
quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-14B FULL(SN-) ASR: SafeNeuron 56/313 vs RLHF-Safety 255/313 vs base 270/313
LLaMA-3.2-8B FULL(SN-) ASR: SafeNeuron 54/313 vs RLHF 142/313 vs base 221/313
Qwen2.5-1.5B ORI ASR: SafeNeuron 1/313 vs RLHF 5/313 vs base 60/313; FULL(SN-) 137 vs 175
Iterative Qwen2.5-7B ASR T0->T3: 279 -> 183 -> 169 -> 158 out of 313
Iterative Qwen2.5-7B frozen safety-neuron count T0->T3: 3717 -> 4848 -> 4953 -> 5218 (capability ARC/GSM8K/TruthfulQA stable)
Qwen2.5-VL-7B VL-Question ASR: SafeNeuron ORI 1/313, FULL 106/313 vs RLHF ORI 0/313, FULL 152/313
Qwen2.5-VL-7B NSFW ASR: SafeNeuron ORI 6/313, FULL 148/313 vs RLHF ORI 35/313, FULL 149/313
Cross-task safety-neuron overlap converges to 16.8% (from ~53.2% at few tasks) at K~6-10 of ten HarmfulQA categories
DeepSeek-R1-1.5B ORI ASR ~257/313 (SafeNeuron), method largely ineffective on this reasoning model
Capability preserved: e.g. Qwen2.5-14B ARC 0.7150 (SafeNeuron) vs 0.7184 (base); GSM8K 0.8105 vs 0.7915
baselines_compared: SN-Tune (Zhao et al. 2025 -- enhances/amplifies identified safety-critical components)
RLHF-Safety (conventional behavioral DPO alignment, Rafailov et al. 2023)
